meta.title: Pain Scale Calculator — NRS, VAS, Wong-Baker | Health Calculators
meta.description: Rate pain intensity using the NRS (0–10), VAS (0–100 mm), and Wong-Baker FACES scales. IASP/WHO classification. Free, instant, anonymous.

painScale.title: Pain Scale Calculator
painScale.description: Rate your pain intensity with three validated clinical scales — NRS, VAS, and Wong-Baker. Returns a category aligned with IASP/WHO standards.

scaleHints.nrs: Numeric Rating Scale — pick a whole number from 0 to 10.
scaleHints.vas: Visual Analog Scale — position on a 100 mm line (0 = no pain, 100 = worst pain).

painScale.incompleteHint: Pick a scale and enter a value to classify your pain intensity.

hints.none: 0/10: No pain. No treatment needed. Stay alert to changes — chronic pain often goes unnoticed in early stages.
hints.mild: 1–3/10: Mild pain. Daily activities are barely affected. Self-care (heat, cold, movement) usually suffices; medication is rarely needed.
hints.moderate: 4–6/10: Moderate pain. Activity is noticeably limited. Medication may be appropriate. If pain persists more than a week, see a clinician.
hints.severe: 7–10/10: Severe pain. Daily life is clearly impaired. See a clinician — and seek immediate care if pain is sudden or accompanied by other symptoms.

painScale.howItWorksText: Three clinically validated pain scales are used here: NRS (Numeric Rating Scale, 0–10), VAS (Visual Analog Scale, 0–100 mm) and Wong-Baker FACES (a faces scale for children and people with language barriers). All values are normalised to a 0–10 scale and binned per Serlin et al. 1995 (IASP/WHO): 0 no pain, 1–3 mild, 4–6 moderate, 7–10 severe.
painScale.disclaimer: This is a self-assessment and does not replace medical diagnosis. For sudden, persistent, or symptom-accompanied pain, seek medical help promptly.

faq.q: What is the NRS pain scale?
faq.a: The Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) is an 11-point scale from 0 to 10. 0 means no pain, 10 means worst pain imaginable. It is the most widely used pain scale in clinical practice worldwide.

faq.q: How does VAS differ from NRS?
faq.a: The Visual Analog Scale (VAS) is an unmarked 100 mm line. Patients mark their pain intensity as a position on the line. VAS offers finer resolution but is more cumbersome to administer.
The FACES scale was developed in 1983 for children aged 3 and older, and is now standard for adults with language barriers or cognitive impairment. Six faces show a 0–10 scale in 2-point steps.
Pain ≥ 4/10 is treatment-worthy in most guidelines. A reduction of at least 30 % or 2 points is considered a clinically meaningful improvement.

faq.q: How often should I rate my pain?
faq.a: Acute pain: rate before and 30–60 minutes after each intervention. Chronic pain: daily at the same time of day, ideally with a pain diary over at least two weeks.

faq.q: What's the difference between pain and suffering?
faq.a: Pain is the sensory component; suffering covers the emotional and functional burden. NRS only measures intensity — for full assessment use multidimensional tools such as the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI).
Yes — self-ratings are the gold standard because pain is subjective. Observer-rated scales are reserved for patients who cannot communicate (e.g. dementia, unconsciousness).
